name: Computer Science Fundamentals — Structured Assessment
description: A fully structured, auto-gradable quiz covering hardware, logic, and sequencing using only fixed-shape question types.
instructions:
Instructions:

Answer every question — each has a single correct, verifiable answer.
Read carefully before selecting, matching, or ordering your response.

items.question: Which part of a computer is considered its 'brain'?
Which sensor would you use to detect distance to an object?
Which of these is an output device?
RAM stores data permanently even when the power is off.
A motor converts electrical energy into motion.
Match each component to its function.
A ___ converts a physical quantity like light or sound into an electrical signal.
In one sentence, explain the difference between RAM and storage (e.g. a hard drive).
items.correctAnswer: RAM is temporary, fast memory used while running programs; storage holds data permanently.

items.question: Which of these is a valid loop structure in most programming languages?
Which of these represents conditional logic?
A sequence in programming means the order in which instructions are executed.
Put the steps of a simple decision-making program in the correct order.
sequence: If true, run the first action
If false, run the alternative action

items.question: A loop that repeats a fixed number of times is called a ___ loop.
Match each programming term to its meaning.
Why is decomposition (breaking a problem into smaller parts) useful in programming?
items.correctAnswer: It makes complex problems easier to solve, test, and manage.

name: Computer Science Fundamentals — Unstructured & Submission Assessment
description: An entirely open-ended, human-graded quiz — every item is either a written/reflective response or a real submission (document, image, video, audio, code, or link).
instructions:
Instructions:

There are no single 'correct' answers here — respond thoughtfully and completely.
For upload items, submit the file type requested; for the link item, include a short note on why you chose it.

items.question: Describe, in detail, how a simple traffic light system could be programmed using sequences and loops.
Write a short essay on how computers have changed the way we learn, referencing at least two examples.
Reflect on a time you solved a problem by breaking it into smaller steps. What did you learn?
A robot keeps bumping into walls even though it has a distance sensor. What could be going wrong, and how would you investigate it?
Using any materials available, build a simple structure that can hold a small weight, and describe your design choices.
What excites you most about learning computer science, and why?
Explain the concept of 'debugging' in your own words, using an example.
Upload a written plan describing how you would build a line-following robot.
Take a photo of a hand-drawn flowchart showing a simple decision-making process, and upload it.
Record a short video (under 2 minutes) demonstrating and explaining a project you have built.
Record an audio clip explaining, in your own words, what an algorithm is.
Upload the source code file for a simple program that prints your name.
Share a link to an online resource (article or video) that helped you understand a computing concept, along with a short note on why it helped.
Upload a document listing three real-world uses of robotics.
Upload a labeled diagram of the components inside a simple robot.

name: Computer Science Fundamentals — Mixed Assessment
description: A blended assessment that pairs auto-gradable structured questions with open-ended reasoning and real submissions, covering the full breadth of item kinds in one quiz.
instructions:
Instructions:

Section 1 has single correct answers — be precise.
Section 2 has no fixed answer — explain your thinking.
Section 3 asks you to submit real work — follow each item's format requirement.

items.question: Which of these best describes an algorithm?
options: A step-by-step set of instructions to solve a problem
items.correctAnswer: A step-by-step set of instructions to solve a problem

items.question: Every algorithm must eventually stop (terminate).
Match each term to its definition.

pairs.right: Steps that transform input into output

items.question: A condition that is either true or false is called a ___ statement.
Arrange the general problem-solving process in order.
What is the purpose of testing a program before using it?
items.correctAnswer: To find and fix errors before the program is used for real.

items.question: Your robot's motor works when tested alone, but stops working once attached to the chassis. Suggest two possible causes.
Explain how sensors, a controller, and motors work together in a robot.
What was the most challenging part of a project you've worked on, and how did you overcome it?
Discuss the impact of automation and robotics on everyday jobs, giving at least two examples.
If you could build any robot, what would it do and why?
Upload a photo or diagram of a system you designed (robot, flowchart, or circuit).
Upload the code for a simple program you wrote (any language).
Record a short video demo of your project in action.
Upload a short written report (one page) describing your project and what you learned.
Share a link to any online tool or tutorial you used while working on your project.

name: Computer Science Fundamentals — Capstone Project
description: A multi-week build project where learners plan, build, test, and present a computing or robotics project of their choice, covering every unstructured and submission-based task type.
instructions:
Instructions:

Complete the Planning tasks before you begin building.
Use the Build & Test tasks to guide your work sessions and submissions.
Check the Deliverables and Milestones tabs for what must be handed in and when.
overview: Design, build, and present a project of your choice that applies programming or robotics concepts to solve a real problem. You will plan your approach, document your build process, and demonstrate a working result.

items.question: Describe your project idea and the problem it solves.
Write a project proposal explaining your goals, audience, and expected outcome.
Reflect on a similar project or system you've seen before starting. What would you do differently?
Imagine your project fails halfway through testing. Describe your plan B.
Sketch your initial design on paper or in a drawing tool.
What is the most exciting part of this project for you?
Upload your detailed build plan.
Upload photos of your project at each build stage.
Record a demo video of your finished project working.
Record an audio walkthrough explaining how your project works.
Upload all source code files for your project.
Share a link to any external tool, library, or reference you used.

rubric.description: How well thought-out and documented the design is.
Whether the project works as intended.
Cleanliness and organization of code or physical construction.
Clarity of the demo video and supporting documentation.
Depth of reflection on the process, challenges, and what was learned.

deliverables.name: Final Build Plan
deliverables.description: The completed, detailed plan used to guide construction.

deliverables.name: Working Demo Video
deliverables.description: A video showing the finished project in action.

deliverables.name: Source Code Archive
deliverables.description: All source files, zipped or linked.

milestones.name: Proposal Approved
milestones.description: Teacher reviews and signs off on the project proposal before building starts.

milestones.name: Prototype Complete
milestones.description: A first working version exists, even if incomplete.

milestones.name: Final Submission
milestones.description: All deliverables submitted and project presented.

name: Computer Science Fundamentals — Take-Home Assignment
description: A blended take-home assignment combining quick concept checks, written reasoning tasks, and real submissions of working code.
instructions:
Instructions:

Complete the Concept Check questions first.
Answer the Written Tasks in your own words.
Submit all files requested in the final section.

items.question: Which loop is best suited when you don't know in advance how many times to repeat?
Functions help avoid repeating the same code multiple times.
A named container that stores a value which can change is called a ___.
Match each symbol to its meaning in pseudocode.
Order the steps to write and run a simple program.
Explain, step by step, how you would design a program to calculate the average of a list of numbers.
Discuss why readable code (good variable names, comments) matters, with examples.
What part of programming do you still find confusing, and why?
Your program crashes when a user enters text instead of a number. How would you handle this?
Using pseudocode or a flowchart, plan a program that checks if a number is even or odd.
Upload the source code for a program that calculates the average of a list of numbers.
Upload a written explanation of your solution and any challenges you faced.
Upload a photo or screenshot of your flowchart or pseudocode plan.
Record a short video walking through your code and explaining how it works.
Share a link to any reference or tutorial you used to complete this assignment.

rubric.description: Does the logic or solution actually work as intended?
How clearly the written tasks are explained.
Readability and organization of submitted code.
All parts of the assignment were attempted.

name: Robotics & Programming Classroom Observation
description: A comprehensive observation checklist for robotics and programming lessons, covering collaboration, problem-solving, and practical/technical skills using every observation item kind.
instructions:
Instructions:

Observe the learner during a robotics or programming session.
Rate each item based on what you directly observe, not what the learner says.
Use the notes items to record anything noteworthy that isn't covered by the checklist or ratings.

indicators.text: Shares materials and tools with teammates without being asked
Communicates ideas clearly during group discussions
Listens to and considers others' ideas before responding
Takes turns and waits patiently during group activities
Attempts to troubleshoot problems independently before asking for help
Breaks a complex problem into smaller, manageable steps
Stays calm and persistent when a solution doesn't work on the first try
Asks clarifying questions when instructions are unclear
Assembles physical components (robot parts, circuits) correctly and securely
Follows multi-step build or coding instructions accurately
Uses tools and equipment safely and appropriately
Stays engaged and on-task during hands-on activities

items.question:
Assignment

Now that you're familiar with the PictoBlox interface and the basics of coding, let's create a fun animation. We'll add a dove, give it a beautiful forest background, and then code it to move across the screen!As you complete this assignment, you will learn important programming ideas like following steps in order, using loops to repeat actions, and starting your code with events. You’ll be using the PictoBlox program to do all of this!

Steps to Complete the Project

Open PictoBlox Software on the blocks Interface by clicking Blocks.

Set Up The Environment

Delete Tobi by clicking the little 'x' on his picture.

Now, add a dove sprite to your project from the sprite library.

Next, you will click the 'Choose a Backdrop' button, then find the forest backdrop where your dove can move through.

Lets Now code

From the yellow Events blocks, drag the when flag clicked block into your coding area.

Next, from the orange Control blocks, snap the forever block right below when flag clicked. This makes sure your dove's actions happen again and again.

Find the blue Motion blocks. Drag the move (10) steps block and place it inside the forever block. This tells your dove to move forward.

Still in the Motion blocks, add the if on edge, bounce block right after move (10) steps, also inside forever. This makes your dove turn around when it hits the edge of the screen, so it doesn't just disappear!

Lastly from the blue motion blocks, drag the set rotation style (left-right) block from the Motion blocks. Put this block below when flag clicked, but outside the forever loop.This keeps your dove from moving upside down.

The project is done!

Click the green flag to see out dove moving!

Save the project as Dove moving

Upload the Assignment.
